questions.text: Lymphocyte B and T have been named due to their:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Relationship with bursa of Fabricius and thymus gland respectively
options.explanation: B lymphocytes are named after the bursa of Fabricius, where they were first discovered in birds, and T lymphocytes are named for their maturation in the thymus gland. This is why they are called B and T lymphocytes.

options.text: Origin from bursa Fabricius and thymus, respectively
options.explanation: While B lymphocytes were discovered in the bursa of Fabricius in birds, T lymphocytes do not originate from the thymus; they mature there. This makes the statement partially correct.

options.text: Storage in bursa of Fabricius and thymus
options.explanation: This is incorrect because lymphocytes are not stored in these organs. The bursa of Fabricius and thymus are involved in the discovery and maturation of B and T lymphocytes, respectively.
This is incorrect as the bursa of Fabricius is not involved in the destruction of lymphocytes. It is related to the discovery of B lymphocytes in birds.

questions.explanation: B cells were first discovered in the bursa of Fabricius (birds), and T cells mature in thymus.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a part of the immune system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phagocytes are a type of white blood cell that plays an important role in the immune system by engulfing and destroying foreign invaders and cellular debris.
Complement proteins are a group of proteins in the blood that work together with other parts of the immune system to destroy foreign invaders.
Cytokines are signaling molecules that help coordinate the immune response and play a key role in the regulation of the immune system.
Atherosclerosis is a disease caused by high levels of cholesterol in the blood, so this is not a part of the immune system

questions.explanation: Atherosclerosis is a disease caused by high levels of cholesterol in the blood. The immune system comprises cells such as lymphocytes, neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils, and phagocytes such as macrophages. It also has complement proteins that help in combating the incoming pathogen. Cytokines released act to promote an immune response to the pathogen in the form of inflammation.

questions.text: The chemical nature of an antibody is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct option. Antibodies are a type of protein known as immunoglobulins. They consist of four polypeptide chains—two heavy chains and two light chains—held together by disulfide bonds. The heavy chains and light chains are composed of amino acids and are often modified by the addition of carbohydrates (sugars) in a process called glycosylation. These sugar modifications make antibodies glycoproteins.

Glycolipids are lipids (fats) that have attached carbohydrates. While glycolipids are involved in various biological processes, they are not directly associated with antibodies. Antibodies are primarily composed of proteins, not lipids.

Lipoproteins are complexes of proteins and lipids. They play a crucial role in transporting lipids, such as cholesterol, in the bloodstream. However, antibodies do not fall under the category of lipoproteins. As mentioned earlier, antibodies are glycoproteins, not lipoproteins.

Polysaccharides are complex carbohydrates composed of multiple sugar units. While antibodies may have sugar modifications (glycosylation) in the form of glycoproteins, they are not primarily composed of polysaccharides. Proteins, rather than polysaccharides, are the main building blocks of antibodies.

questions.explanation: Antibodies are a type of protein known as immunoglobulins. They consist of four polypeptide chains—two heavy chains and two light chains—held together by disulfide bonds. The heavy chains and light chains are composed of amino acids and are often modified by the addition of carbohydrates (sugars) in a process called glycosylation. These sugar modifications make antibodies glycoproteins. Antibodies can be described as glycoproteins, compounds that consist of carbohydrates and proteins.

questions.text: Thymus gland provides immunological competence to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: B-lymphocytes are generated and mature in the bone marrow, independent of the thymus.
Antibodies are produced by plasma cells, which originate from B-lymphocytes, not directly influenced by the thymus gland.
T-lymphocytes mature in the thymus gland, where they acquire immunological competence.
Immunoglobulins, also known as antibodies, are produced by plasma cells and are not a direct product of the thymus gland's function.

questions.explanation: The thymus gland is crucial for the maturation of T-lymphocytes, which are essential components of the adaptive immune system. In the thymus, T-lymphocytes undergo a process that equips them with the ability to recognize and respond to specific antigens, thus achieving immunological competence. On the other hand, B-lymphocytes are produced and mature in the bone marrow, where they differentiate independently of the thymus. Antibodies and immunoglobulins are produced by B-lymphocyte-derived plasma cells and do not directly involve the thymus in their production or function.

questions.text: An antibody molecule consists of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because an antibody molecule consists of more than just light chains; it also includes heavy chains.
This option is incorrect because an antibody molecule not only has heavy chains but also includes light chains.

options.text: Two identical light and two identical heavy chains
options.explanation: This option is correct. An antibody molecule is composed of two identical light chains and two identical heavy chains, forming a Y-shaped structure.

options.text: Two identical light chains and two non-identical heavy chains
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because both the light chains and the heavy chains in an antibody molecule are identical to each other.

questions.explanation: An antibody molecule, also known as an immunoglobulin, is a Y-shaped protein that plays a critical role in the immune response by recognizing and binding to antigens. Its structure includes two identical light chains and two identical heavy chains, which are connected by disulfide bonds. This configuration allows antibodies to specifically bind to antigens. Option A is incorrect because it only mentions light chains. Option B is incorrect because it only mentions heavy chains. Option D is incorrect because it suggests the heavy chains are non-identical, which is not the case.

questions.text: In light chain of antibodies:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The variable sequence of amino acids is longer.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The variable region is critical for antigen binding and needs to be adaptable, but it is not necessarily longer than the constant region.

options.text: The variable sequence of amino acids is shorter.
options.explanation: The variable region is typically shorter in terms of its constant sequence counterparts, but its diversity allows for a wide range of antigen recognition.

options.text: Both variable and constant amino acid sequences have equal length.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The lengths can vary, and the variable region is specifically organized to accommodate diverse sequences for antigen binding.

options.text: Only the variable sequence of amino acids occurs.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Both variable and constant regions are present in the light chain of antibodies, each serving distinct roles.

questions.explanation: The light chain of an antibody consists of both variable and constant regions. The variable region is shorter in terms of sequence length but is highly diverse to enable the recognition of a wide variety of antigens, which is crucial for the adaptive immune response. The constant region provides structural stability. Option B is correct because it correctly identifies the typical structure of the light chain, while the other options either misinterpret the structural differences or incorrectly describe the presence or absence of these regions.

questions.text: Globular blood proteins that are produced by B-lymphocytes and that bind specifically to foreign antigenic materials in the body and destroy them are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In immunology, an antigen is a molecule, moiety, foreign particulate matter, or an allergen, such as pollen, that can bind to a specific antibody or T-cell receptor. The presence of antigens in the body may trigger an immune response. Antigens can be proteins, peptides, polysaccharides, lipids, or nucleic acids.

An immunogen is a molecule capable of eliciting an immune response when injected into an animal, not to be confused with an antigen, which is capable of binding to an antibody but not necessarily eliciting an immune response. An immunogen and antigen may have many different sites at which antibodies may bind.

An antibody, also known as an immunoglobulin, is a large, Y-shaped protein used by the immune system to identify and neutralize foreign objects such as pathogenic bacteria and viruses. The antibody recognizes a unique molecule of the pathogen, called an antigen.
Antibiotics are medicinal drugs to combat bacterial diseases.

questions.explanation: An antibody (Ab), also known as an immunoglobulin (Ig), is a large Y-shaped protein produced mainly by plasma cells that is used by the immune system to identify and neutralize pathogens such as bacteria and viruses. The antibody recognizes a unique molecule of the harmful agent, called antigen, via variable region.

questions.text: The cells which play very important role in developing immunity are except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Monocytes play a very important role in developing immunity. Monocytes are the largest type of white blood cell, and they are produced in the bone marrow. After circulating in the blood for a few days, monocytes migrate to tissues, where they mature into macrophages. Macrophages are phagocytic cells that can engulf and destroy bacteria, viruses, and other foreign invaders.

Lymphocytes are the cells that play a very important role in developing immunity. They are a type of white blood cell that is responsible for producing antibodies and mediating the adaptive immune response. There are two main types of lymphocytes: B cells and T cells.B cells produce antibodies, which are proteins that bind to specific antigens on the surface of foreign invaders. When an antibody binds to an antigen, it triggers a cascade of events that ultimately leads to the destruction of the invader.T cells are responsible for mediating the adaptive immune response. There are many different types of T cells, but they all play a role in fighting infection. Some T cells help B cells to produce antibodies, while others directly attack and kill infected cells.

Neutrophils play a very important role in developing immunity. They are the most abundant type of white blood cell in the body, and they are the first to respond to a bacterial infection. Neutrophils are phagocytic cells, which means they can engulf and destroy bacteria. They do this by releasing enzymes and chemicals that kill the bacteria. Neutrophils also release cytokines, which are proteins that help to recruit other immune cells to the site of infection. Neutrophils are produced in the bone marrow and mature in the bloodstream. They have a lifespan of about 7 days. When a neutrophil encounters a bacteria, it will engulf the bacteria and kill it. The neutrophil will then die and release its contents, which will attract other neutrophils to the site of infection.

Thrombocytes do not play a role in developing immunity. Thrombocytes, also known as platelets, are blood cells that are responsible for blood clotting. They do not have a nucleus and are small, disc-shaped cells. Thrombocytes are produced in the bone marrow and circulate in the blood. The immune system is a complex network of cells, tissues, and organs that work together to protect the body from infection. The immune system has two main types of responses: innate immunity and adaptive immunity.Innate immunity is the body's first line of defense against infection. It is made up of cells and molecules that are always present in the body and are ready to fight off infection. Innate immune cells include neutrophils, macrophages, and natural killer cells.Adaptive immunity is the body's second line of defense against infection. It is slower to respond than innate immunity, but it is more specific and can remember specific infections. Adaptive immune cells include B cells and T cells. Thrombocytes do not play a role in either innate or adaptive immunity. Their only function is to help the body clot blood.

questions.explanation: Thrombocytes (platelets) are for clotting, not immunity. Immune defense relies on lymphocytes, neutrophils, and monocytes.

questions.text: Antibodies are manufactured in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, antibodies are not manufactured in T lymphocytes. Antibodies are manufactured in B lymphocytes.T lymphocytes, also known as T cells, are another type of white blood cell that plays a key role in the body's immune system. T cells are responsible for helping the body to fight infection by killing infected cells and by helping B cells to produce antibodies.

No, antibodies are not manufactured in platelets. Antibodies are proteins that are produced by B cells in the bone marrow. Platelets are small, disk-shaped cells that are involved in blood clotting. They do not produce antibodies.

No, antibodies are not manufactured in red blood cells (RBCs). RBCs are the most abundant type of blood cell. They are responsible for carrying oxygen from the lungs to the tissues. RBCs do not have a nucleus and are therefore unable to produce proteins, including antibodies.

Antibodies are manufactured in B lymphocytes. B lymphocytes are a type of white blood cell that are responsible for the body's humoral immune response. The humoral immune response is the part of the immune system that fights off infection by producing antibodies. Antibodies are proteins that bind to specific antigens, which are molecules found on the surface of foreign invaders. When an antibody binds to an antigen, it triggers a cascade of events that ultimately leads to the destruction of the foreign invader. B lymphocytes are produced in the bone marrow. When a B lymphocyte encounters an antigen for the first time, it becomes activated. The activated B lymphocyte then divides and produces a clone of cells that all produce the same antibody. These cells are called plasma cells. Plasma cells secrete antibodies into the blood and lymph fluid.

questions.explanation: Antibodies are manufactured in B lymphocytes. B lymphocytes are a type of white blood cell that are responsible for the body's humoral immune response. The humoral immune response is the part of the immune system that fights off infection by producing antibodies. Antibodies are proteins that bind to specific antigens, which are molecules found on the surface of foreign invaders. When an antibody binds to an antigen, it triggers a cascade of events that ultimately leads to the destruction of the foreign invader. B lymphocytes are produced in the bone marrow. When a B lymphocyte encounters an antigen for the first time, it becomes activated. The activated B lymphocyte then divides and produces a clone of cells that all produce the same antibody. These cells are called plasma cells. Plasma cells secrete antibodies into the blood and lymph fluid.

questions.text: All of the following protect the body against entrance of germs except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mucus membranes do help protect the body against the entrance of germs. They do this in a few ways:Mucus membranes trap germs and prevent them from entering the body. Mucus is a sticky substance that is produced by mucus membranes. It helps to trap germs and other particles that enter the body through the nose, mouth, and other openings. The mucus is then moved out of the body by the action of cilia, which are tiny hair-like structures that line the mucus membranes.Mucus membranes contain antimicrobial substances that kill germs. Mucus membranes also contain antimicrobial substances, such as lysozyme and immunoglobulin A (IgA), that help to kill germs. Lysozyme is an enzyme that breaks down the cell walls of bacteria, while IgA is an antibody that helps to bind to and neutralize bacteria.Mucus membranes produce white blood cells that fight infection. Mucus membranes also contain white blood cells, which are the body's first line of defense against infection. The white blood cells move to the site of infection and help to kill the germs.

WBCs (white blood cells) do help protect the body against the entrance of germs. They do this in a few ways:WBCs patrol the body for foreign invaders. WBCs are constantly circulating in the blood and lymph fluid, looking for foreign invaders. When they find a germ, they attach to it and engulf it.WBCs produce antibodies that attack germs. Antibodies are proteins that bind to specific antigens, which are molecules found on the surface of germs. When an antibody binds to an antigen, it triggers a cascade of events that ultimately leads to the destruction of the germ.WBCs release chemicals that kill germs. WBCs release chemicals, such as defensins and cytokines, that can kill germs directly.

Ciliated cells do help protect the body against the entrance of germs. They do this by moving mucus and trapped germs out of the body. Ciliated cells are found in the lining of the respiratory tract, including the nose, throat, and lungs. They have tiny hair-like projections called cilia that beat rhythmically to move mucus and trapped germs up and out of the airways. This process is called mucociliary clearance.

RBCs (red blood cells) do not protect the body against the entrance of germs. RBCs are responsible for carrying oxygen to the tissues and carbon dioxide away from the tissues. They do not have the ability to engulf or kill germs.

questions.explanation: RBCs have no role in immunity. RBCs (red blood cells) do not protect the body against the entrance of germs. RBCs are responsible for carrying oxygen to the tissues and carbon dioxide away from the tissues. They cannot engulf or kill germs.

questions.text: Antivenom injection contains preformed antibodies while polio drops that are administered into the body contain:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the Sabin vaccine or Polio vaccine consists of attenuated viral strains, not gamma globulin.
This option is correct because the Sabin vaccine or polio vaccine consists of attenuated viral strains.
This option is incorrect because the Sabin vaccine or Polio vaccine consists of attenuated viral strains, not activated pathogens.
This option is incorrect because the Sabin vaccine or Polio vaccine consists of attenuated viral strains, not harvested antibodies.

questions.explanation: The oral polio vaccine (OPV) is administered in the form of polio drops and typically contains weakened, live poliovirus strains. These weakened poliovirus strains are designed to be incapable of causing the disease but can stimulate the immune system to produce an immune response, including the production of antibodies. In some regions, an inactivated polio vaccine (IPV) is also used, which contains inactivated (killed) poliovirus and is typically administered as an injection.

questions.text: The antigen-antibody complexes formed in the body are taken up by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phagocytes (neutrophils and monocytes) are immune cells that play a critical role in both the early and late stages of immune responses.

A lymphocyte is a type of white blood cell in the immune system of most vertebrates. Lymphocytes include T cells, B cells, and Innate lymphoid cells, of which natural killer cells are an important subtype.

Monocytes are a type of leukocyte or white blood cell. They are the largest type of leukocyte in blood and can differentiate into macrophages and monocyte-derived dendritic cells. As a part of the vertebrate innate immune system monocytes also influence adaptive immune responses and exert tissue repair functions.

White blood cells, or leukocytes (Greek; leucko=white and cyte = cell), are part of the immune system participating in both the innate and humoral immune responses. They circulate in the blood and mount inflammatory and cellular responses to injury or pathogens.

questions.explanation: Phagocytes, such as neutrophils and macrophages, are crucial for the immune system's function of engulfing and digesting foreign particles, including antigen-antibody complexes. These cells are equipped to identify and internalize these complexes, aiding in their removal from the body. While lymphocytes are vital for recognizing antigens and initiating immune responses, they do not perform phagocytosis. Monocytes can differentiate into macrophages, which are phagocytic, but the term 'monocytes' itself does not directly imply phagocytic activity. Leukocytes encompass a broader category of white blood cells, not all of which are phagocytic.

questions.text: The T-lymphocytes and B-lymphocytes are the major cells of the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The thymus plays a major role in the maturation of B and T lymphocytes; however, they are not the major cells of the thymus. Hence, this option is incorrect.
B and T lymphocytes are one of the major cells in lymph nodes. They play an important role in the immune system. Hence, this option is the correct one.
B lymphocytes and T lymphocytes are not major cells of the adrenal glands; rather, their major cells are called steroidogenic cells and chromaffin cells. Hence, this option is incorrect.
The major cells of lymphatic vessels are lymphatic endothelial cells, not B and T lymphocytes. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Lymph nodes house large populations of B and T lymphocytes for immune defense.

questions.text: Which one of the following gives rise to macrophages?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Neutrophils have a very short life span and only initiate the reaction by direct destruction of pathogen, hence they can not give rise to macrophages.

Eosinophils have a rather longer life span and they get attracted to places where there is inflammation, hence large amounts of it can be shown in a blood test and allow us to know that there is an infection.

Monocytes have a rather longer life span and take a more intensive approach to killing the pathogen compared to the neutrophils. They are also able to give rise to macrophages, hence it is the correct option.

There are 2 types of lymphocytes, B and T lymphocytes. They both are made in the bone marrow, but B lymphocyte matures in it whereas T lymphocyte matures in the thymus gland. They both are white blood cells and they kill the pathogen by attacking it with the help of its killer cells. They do not give rise to macrophages, therefore it is a wrong option.

questions.explanation: Monocytes have a relatively longer lifespan and employ a more intensive approach to killing pathogens compared to neutrophils. They are also able to give rise to macrophages, hence it is the correct option.

questions.text: The cells that play a very important role in developing immunity are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is a part of the innate immune system. They phagocytose (eat) pathogens and later differentiate into macrophages or dendritic cells. They help initiate immune responses but do not generate memory or specificity.

Neutrophils are not involved in developing immunity because they are part of the innate immune system and act in a non-specific, short-term manner. They respond quickly to infections by engulfing and destroying pathogens, but do not recognize specific invaders or form memory cells.
Lymphocytes play a very important role in developing immunity (long-term), unlike other cells, like monocytes and neutrophils, which are nonspecific and provide short-term immunity.

Thrombocytes, also known as platelets, are small cell fragments in the blood that play a crucial role in blood clotting. They are essential for wound healing and preventing excessive bleeding by forming blood clots at the site of vascular injury.

questions.explanation: Lymphocytes play a very important role in developing immunity (long-term), unlike other cells, like monocytes and neutrophils, which are nonspecific and provide short-term immunity. Thrombocytes, also known as platelets, are involved in blood clotting. Monocytes are not the primary cells responsible for "developing" immunity. Neutrophils are crucial for immediate defense, but they do not play a role in developing long-term, specific immunity

questions.text: Antibodies are actually:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Antibodies are globular proteins that play a key role in the immune system by binding to antigens. Their compact, three-dimensional structure enables them to specifically recognize and neutralize foreign substances.

Glycoproteins are proteins that have carbohydrate chains (glycans) attached to them. While some proteins in the body, including antibodies, can be glycosylated (have carbohydrates attached), the primary description for antibodies is their classification as globular proteins.

Fibrous proteins have elongated, thread-like shapes and are often involved in structural roles. Examples of fibrous proteins include collagen and keratin. Antibodies do not fall under the category of fibrous proteins due to their globular shape and function.

Glycolipids are molecules composed of both carbohydrates and lipids. They are typically found in cell membranes and serve various roles, including cell recognition. Antibodies are not glycolipids; they are globular proteins involved in immune responses.

questions.explanation: Antibodies are globular proteins that play a key role in the immune system by binding to antigens. Their compact, three-dimensional structure enables them to specifically recognize and neutralize foreign substances.

questions.text: T-Lymphocytes are matured in the thymus glands, and they are produced in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect as T-lymphocytes originate from the stem cells of bone marrow, and later they are transported to the thymus gland for further processing.
The option is correct as T-lymphocytes originate from the stem cells of bone marrow, and later they are transported to the thymus gland for further processing.
The option is incorrect as T-lymphocytes are not produced in the pancreas.
The option is incorrect as the heart produces no hormones.

questions.explanation: T-lymphocytes originate from stem cells in bone marrow, then migrate to the thymus gland for maturation.

questions.text: The system responsible for fighting the pathogens is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The muscular system is responsible for movement and locomotion in the body. While it plays a crucial role in various bodily functions, it is not directly involved in fighting pathogens or infections.

The endocrine system consists of various glands that produce hormones and regulate bodily functions. While hormones can influence immune responses, the endocrine system itself is not primarily responsible for directly fighting pathogens.

The nervous system is responsible for transmitting signals and coordinating communication between different parts of the body. It controls voluntary and involuntary actions but does not directly participate in fighting pathogens.

The immune system is a complex network of cells, tissues, and organs that work together to defend the body against pathogens, such as bacteria, viruses, and fungi. It recognizes and eliminates foreign invaders, produces antibodies, and activates immune responses to prevent and control infections. The immune system includes various components, such as white blood cells (leukocytes), lymphoid tissues (like the spleen and lymph nodes), and specialized molecules like antibodies. It has both innate and adaptive immune responses that allow the body to recognize and respond to a wide range of pathogens.

questions.explanation: The immune system defends against infections using innate (nonspecific) and adaptive (specific) mechanisms.

questions.text: An example of passive acquired immunity is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The polio vaccine is an example of artificially acquired active immunity.

options.text: Passing of certain antibodies to the foetus by the pregnant women
options.explanation: Antibodies from a mother can cross the placenta and enter her foetus. This is an example of natural passive immunity.

options.text: Inoculation of antitoxin in the case of puncture wound
options.explanation: Passive immunity is also provided by antitoxins. Thus, this option is also correct
In passive immunity, antibodies from one individual are passed to another person.

questions.explanation: Passive immunity is provided when a person is given antibodies to a disease rather than producing them through his or her own immune system.

questions.text: Humoral immunity is carried by a special group of cells called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Humoral immunity is also called antibody-mediated immunity. B cells differentiate into plasma B cells that can produce antibodies against a specific antigen.

Option B is wrong as killer cells or natural killer cells are a type of immune cell that have granules with enzymes that can kill tumor cells or virally infected cells. They do not exhibit humoral immunity.
Option C is wrong as helper cells (T-helper cells) are involved in cell-mediated immunity.
Option D is wrong as null cells are large granular lymphocytes produced in the bone marrow and attack pathogens and toxic substances.

questions.explanation: Humoral immunity is also called antibody-mediated immunity. B-cells differentiate into plasma B cells that can produce antibodies against a specific antigen.

questions.text: All of the following protect the body against the entrance of germs except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tears are physical barriers (part of the first line of defense ) that protect our body from germs.
Mucous membranes are physical barriers (part of the first line of defense) that protect our body from germs.
Ciliated cells are physical barriers (part of the first line of defense) that protect our body from germs.
Red blood cells facilitate the transport of O2 through hemoglobin within them from the lungs to the body's cells and tissues.

questions.explanation: Red blood cells are responsible for transporting O2 via hemoglobin stored in them from the lungs to the body's cells and tissues. Options A, B, and C are incorrect because they are physical barriers (part of the first line of defense) that protect our body from germs.

questions.text: Anti-bodies are produced by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Red blood cells: Red blood cells, also known as erythrocytes, are responsible for carrying oxygen from the lungs to the body's tissues and carbon dioxide from the tissues to the lungs. They do not produce antibodies.
Platelets: Platelets are small, disc-shaped cell fragments that play a crucial role in blood clotting. They are not involved in antibody production.

B-lymphocytes: B-lymphocytes, also called B-cells, are a type of white blood cell that plays a central role in the adaptive immune response. When a pathogen (like a virus or bacteria) enters the body, B-cells recognize specific antigens (molecules on the surface of the pathogen) and produce antibodies. These antibodies are proteins that bind to the antigens and help neutralize or destroy the pathogen. B-cells are essential for the immune system's ability to remember and respond to previously encountered pathogens, leading to immunity.

Hormones: Hormones are signaling molecules that are produced by various glands in the body's endocrine system. They regulate various physiological processes but are not directly involved in the production of antibodies.

questions.explanation: B-lymphocytes: B-lymphocytes, also called B-cells, are a type of white blood cell that plays a central role in the adaptive immune response. When a pathogen (like a virus or bacteria) enters the body, B-cells recognize specific antigens (molecules on the surface of the pathogen) and produce antibodies. These antibodies are proteins that bind to the antigens and help neutralize or destroy the pathogen. B-cells are essential for the immune system's ability to remember and respond to previously encountered pathogens, leading to immunity. In summary, antibodies are produced by B-lymphocytes as part of the adaptive immune response, which helps the body fight off infections and provides immunity against future encounters with the same pathogen.

questions.text: T- Lymphocyte is named due to its relationship to _ gland, as its influence is essential in making T- cells immunologically competent.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The thyroid gland is responsible for secreting T4 and T3. The thyroid is a small, butterfly-shaped gland located in the front of the neck, just below the Adam's apple. It is an essential part of the endocrine system, which is responsible for producing and releasing hormones that regulate various bodily functions.

The thymus is a specialized gland located in the upper chest, behind the breastbone (sternum), and in front of the heart. It is a crucial part of the immune system, particularly in the development and maturation of T cells, a type of white blood cell that plays a vital role in immune responses. Overall, the thymus gland is essential for the development and education of T cells, which are crucial components of the immune system

Testes are responsible for making sperm and producing testosterone. Testes, also known as the testicles, are the male reproductive organs responsible for the production of sperm and the synthesis of male sex hormones, primarily testosterone. They are located in the scrotum, a sac-like structure outside the body, below the penis.

A bursa is a closed, fluid-filled sac that works as a cushion and gliding surface to reduce friction between tissues of the body. A bursa is a small, fluid-filled sac that acts as a cushion and reduces friction between tissues such as bones, tendons, muscles, and skin. These sacs are found throughout the body in areas where there is frequent movement or rubbing between structures. The plural form of bursa is bursae.

questions.explanation: T- cells are so-called because they are predominantly produced in the thymus. As the names suggest helper T- cells 'help' other cells of the immune system, whilst cytotoxic T- cells kill virally infected cells and tumours.

questions.text: The example of artificially induced active immunity is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Exposure to actual infection is natural active immunity. Exposure to an actual infection typically leads to the development of adaptive immunity, specifically known as acquired immunity or specific immunity.

Antiserum is a blood serum containing monoclonal or polyclonal antibodies that is used to spread passive immunity to many diseases via blood. The injection of antiserum is an example of passive immunity. Antiserum contains preformed antibodies that are specifically targeted against a particular pathogen or toxin. These antibodies are derived from the blood or serum of an individual or animal that has been previously exposed to the pathogen or immunized against it.

Injection of antibiotics is passive immunity. The injection of antibiotics does not confer immunity. Antibiotics are medications used to treat bacterial infections by inhibiting or killing the bacteria causing the infection. They do not stimulate the immune system or provide long-term protection against future infections.

Vaccination provides artificial active immunity. Vaccines are a means of developing active immunity. They stimulate the immune system to recognize and remember specific pathogens, such as viruses or bacteria, without causing the actual disease.

questions.explanation: Artificial active immunity is acquired through the introduction of a killed or weakened form of the disease organism through vaccination.

questions.text: Which one of the following is related to the defense system of the human body?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Red blood cells, also known as erythrocytes, carry oxygen from the lungs to the body's tissues and remove carbon dioxide. While they play a crucial role in the circulatory system, red blood cells are not directly related to the human body's defence system.

White blood cells (also known as leukocytes) are a crucial component of the immune system and are responsible for defending the body against infectious diseases and foreign invaders. Red blood cells and platelets are involved in the transportation of oxygen and clotting of blood, respectively, but do not play a direct role in the defense system. Plasma is the liquid component of blood that contains various proteins, hormones, and nutrients.

Platelets, or thrombocytes, are small cell fragments in the blood that help to clot and prevent excessive bleeding when blood vessels are damaged. While platelets contribute to the body's hemostatic response, they are not directly related to the defense system or immune response.

Plasma is the liquid component of blood that carries various substances, including nutrients, hormones, antibodies, and waste products. It serves as a medium for transporting these substances throughout the body, but is not directly involved in the defense system.

questions.explanation: "White blood cells" (also known as leukocytes) are a crucial component of the immune system and are responsible for defending the body against infectious diseases and foreign invaders."Red blood cells" and "platelets" are involved in the transportation of oxygen and clotting of blood, respectively, but do not play a direct role in the defense system. "Plasma" is the blood's liquid component containing various proteins, hormones, and nutrients. White blood cells conduct their defense activities by ingesting foreign materials and cellular debris, destroying infectious agents and cancer cells, or producing antibodies.

questions.text: Which of the following cells recognize antigens and form plasma cell clones?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It seems that "A cells" is not a commonly used term in the context of immune system cells.

B cells are a type of white blood cell that recognize antigens and produce antibodies in response to them. When activated, B cells can form a clone of plasma cells that produce large amounts of specific antibodies to fight off an infection.
It appears that "F cells" is not a recognized term in the context of immune system cells.

T cells are another type of white blood cell that plays a crucial role in the immune response. T cells are involved in cell-mediated immunity and can recognize specific antigens. However, T cells do not directly form plasma cell clones. Instead, they help coordinate the immune response by activating other immune cells, including B cells, which can then differentiate into plasma cells.

questions.explanation: B lymphocytes recognize antigens and differentiate into plasma cells that secrete antibodies.

questions.text: In White Blood Cells, Monocytes have a short life period of _ hours.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Monocytes are a type of white blood cell that have a short lifespan of about 10-20 hours. They are produced in the bone marrow and then circulate in the blood for a short period of time before moving to tissues, where they mature into macrophages. Macrophages are responsible for cleaning up dead cells and debris, and they also play a role in the immune response.
21-30 hours: This is the lifespan of neutrophils, another type of white blood cell. Neutrophils are responsible for fighting infection, and they have a shorter lifespan than monocytes.

31-35 hours: This is the lifespan of lymphocytes, another type of white blood cell. Lymphocytes are responsible for the adaptive immune response, and they have a longer lifespan than monocytes and neutrophils.
36-40 hours: This is not a typical lifespan for any type of white blood cell. White blood cells typically have a lifespan of hours to days.

questions.explanation: Option A: 10-20 hours: This is the lifespan of monocytes, a type of white blood cell that fights infection.Option B: 21-30 hours: This is the lifespan of neutrophils, another type of white blood cell that fights infection.Option C: 31-35 hours: This is the lifespan of lymphocytes, another type of white blood cell that fights infection.Option D: 36-40 hours: This is not a typical lifespan for any type of white blood cell. White blood cells typically have a lifespan of hours to days.

questions.text: A group of immune cells that mediate the cellular immune response by processing and presenting antigens for recognition of certain other cells or immune systems is called _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Natural killer cells are involved in the direct destruction of virus-infected and cancerous cells, but they do not process or present antigens.
This option is incorrect. Interferons are signaling proteins that help regulate the immune response rather than processing or presenting antigens.

This option is correct. Antigen-presenting cells (APCs) play a crucial role in the immune system by processing and presenting antigens on their surface to be recognized by T-cells, facilitating an adaptive immune response.
This option is incorrect. Vaccines contain antigens or components that stimulate an immune response, but they are not cells that process or present antigens.

questions.explanation: Antigen-presenting cells (APCs) like macrophages and dendritic cells display antigens to T-cells, activating immune response.

questions.text: Cell-mediated immune response is given by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cell-mediated immunity is an immune response that does not involve antibodies. Rather, cell-mediated immunity is the activation of phagocytes, antigen-specific cytotoxic T-lymphocytes, and the release of various cytokines in response to an antigen. Humoral immunity is also called antibody-mediated immunity. With assistance from helper T cells, B cells will differentiate into plasma B cells that can produce antibodies against a specific antigen. The humoral immune system deals with antigens from pathogens that are freely circulating, or outside the infected cells. Antibodies produced by the B cells will bind to antigens, neutralising them, or causing lysis (dissolution or destruction of cells by a lysin) or phagocytosis.

B lymphocytes, or B cells, are a type of white blood cell involved in the immune system. They play a crucial role in the adaptive immune response by producing antibodies, which recognize and neutralize pathogens like bacteria and viruses.

Neutrophils are a type of white blood cell that forms a significant part of the body's immune system. They are essential for fighting bacterial infections and are often the first responders to sites of infection or inflammation.

Macrophages are a type of white blood cell that functions as a part of the immune system. They play a key role in engulfing and digesting cellular debris, foreign substances, and pathogens. Macrophages also contribute to the regulation of immune responses and tissue repair.

questions.explanation: Cell-mediated immunity is an immune response that does not involve antibodies. Rather, cell-mediated immunity is the activation of phagocytes, antigen-specific cytotoxic T-lymphocytes, and the release of various cytokines in response to an antigen. Humoral immunity is also called antibody-mediated immunity. With assistance from helper T cells, B cells will differentiate into plasma B cells that can produce antibodies against a specific antigen. The humoral immune system deals with antigens from pathogens that are freely circulating, or outside the infected cells. Antibodies produced by the B cells will bind to antigens, neutralizing them, or causing lysis (dissolution or destruction of cells by a lysin) or phagocytosis.

questions.text: In immunoglobulins/antibodies, two light chains and two heavy chains are linked to each other by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Covalent bonds: While strong, covalent bonds are not the primary linkage between light and heavy chains in immunoglobulins.
This is incorrect. Hydrogen bonds: These are weaker bonds and contribute to the overall structure, but are not the main force linking the chains.
This is incorrect. Ionic bonds: These bonds can play a role in protein structure, but disulfide bonds are the key link between the light and heavy chains.

Each antibody is made up of two short light (L) peptide chains and two long heavy (H) peptide chains (H2L2) joined to each other by disulfide bonds. Disulfide bonds are covalent linkages between the sulfur-containing side chains of cysteine amino acids. The peptide chains are arranged in a Y-shaped structure. Each chain in the antibody has both constant and variable regions. The amino acid sequence and conformation of the variable region keep changing from one type of antibody to another. The conformation and amino acid sequence of the constant region remain the same. The unique antigen-binding site in each antibody that is compatible with specific antigens is composed of the variable regions of both the heavy and the light chains.

questions.explanation: Each antibody is made up of two short light (L) peptide chains and two long heavy (H) peptide chains (H2L2) joined to each other by disulfide bonds. Disulfide bonds are covalent linkages between the sulphur-containing side chains of cysteine amino acids. The peptide chains are arranged in a Y-shaped structure. Each chain in the antibody has both constant and variable regions. The amino acid sequence and conformation of the variable region keep changing from one type of antibody to another. The conformation and amino acid sequence of the constant region remain the same. The unique antigen-binding site in each antibody that is compatible with specific antigens is composed of the variable regions of both the heavy and the light chains.

questions.text: Which one of the following cells does not have a nucleus:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Eosinophils have a nucleus. Their nucleus is bilobed. These cells inactivate inflammation-producing substances and attack parasites.
Neutrophils have a nucleus that can be two to five-lobed. They destroy small particles by phagocytosis.
Badophils possess a bilobed nucleus. They release heparin to prevent blood clots and histamine, which causes inflammation.

Platelets are irregularly shaped and typically measure only 2-3 micrometres in diameter. Platelets do not have a nucleus as they are not true cells, but are instead classified as cell fragments produced by megakaryocytes. These are involved in blood clotting.Neutrophils, eosinophils, and basophils are all types of white blood cells that contain nuclei.

questions.explanation: Platelets are irregularly shaped and typically measure only 2–3 micrometers in diameter. Platelets do not possess a nucleus as they are not true cells, but are instead classified as cell fragments produced by megakaryocytes. These are involved in blood clotting.Neutrophils, eosinophils, and basophils are all types of white blood cells that contain a nucleus.

questions.text: The most common site for autoimmune disease:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect because not only skin and joints are common sites for autoimmune diseases, but also the muscles
This is incorrect because not only muscles are common sites for autoimmune diseases, but also the skin and joints.
Muscles, skin, and joints are common sites for autoimmune diseases.
This is incorrect because muscles, skin, and joints are common sites for autoimmune diseases.

questions.explanation: Muscles, skins and joints are common sites for autoimmune diseases.

questions.text: In _ response, β-cells produce plasma cells that synthesize antibodies and release them in blood plasma and tissue fluid.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is not the correct response in the given context. Cell-mediated response typically refers to the immune response that involves the activation of immune cells, such as T-cells, to directly attack infected or abnormal cells. It does not involve the production of plasma cells and antibodies as described in the sentence.

This option is not the correct response in the given context. Hormonal response typically refers to the release of hormones by endocrine glands to regulate various physiological processes in the body. It does not specifically involve the production of plasma cells and antibodies.

This option is the correct response in the given context. Humoral response refers to the immune response involving the production of antibodies by plasma cells. In this context, β-cells are producing plasma cells that synthesize antibodies and release them into the blood plasma and tissue fluid. This accurately describes the process mentioned in the sentence.

This option is not the correct response in the given context. Phototactic refers to the movement or orientation of organisms in response to light. It is not relevant to the production of plasma cells and antibodies in the immune response.

questions.explanation: This option is the correct response in the given context. Humoral response refers to the immune response involving the production of antibodies by plasma cells. In this context, β-cells are producing plasma cells that synthesize antibodies and release them into the blood plasma and tissue fluid. This accurately describes the process mentioned in the sentence.

questions.text: B-lymphocytes are named due to their relationship with:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Blood: B-lymphocytes circulate in the blood but are not named after it.
Bone Marrow: In mammals, B-lymphocytes mature in the bone marrow, but the name is not derived from it.
B lymphocytes were first discovered in the Bursa of Fabricius (a structure found in birds), and they are called so with this respect.
Bile Duct: Bile ducts are unrelated to B-lymphocytes and have no role in their development or naming.

questions.explanation: B lymphocytes were first discovered in the Bursa of Fabricius (a structure found in birds), and they are called so with this respect.

questions.text: Antigen is a foreign protein or any other molecule which stimulates the formation of :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) MHC complex: Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC) molecules are proteins found on the surface of cells. They play a crucial role in the immune system by presenting antigens to T cells, which are a type of immune cell. MHC complexes help T cells recognize antigens, but they are not the molecules that stimulate the formation of antibodies.

b) Immunogen: An immunogen is a substance, such as a foreign protein or other molecule, that stimulates the immune system to produce an immune response. When an immunogen enters the body, it triggers the production of antibodies and activates other components of the immune system to fight off the foreign substance.

c) Mucus: Mucus is a thick, slimy substance produced by mucous membranes in the body. It serves as a protective barrier in various organs, including the respiratory and digestive tracts, but it does not stimulate the formation of antibodies.

d) Antibodies: Antibodies, also known as immunoglobulins, are proteins produced by the immune system in response to the presence of antigens. Antibodies specifically bind to antigens, marking them for destruction by immune cells. While antibodies are produced in response to antigens, they are not responsible for stimulating their own formation.

questions.explanation: d) Antibodies: Antibodies, also known as immunoglobulins, are proteins produced by the immune system in response to the presence of antigens. Antibodies specifically bind to antigens, marking them for destruction by immune cells. While antibodies are produced in response to antigens, they are not responsible for stimulating their own formation.

questions.text: Antibodies are produced by which of the following lymphocytes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) B lymphocytes: This is the correct answer. B lymphocytes, also known as B cells, are a type of white blood cell that is responsible for producing antibodies. When B cells encounter antigens, they differentiate into plasma cells, which secrete large quantities of antibodies specific to the antigen.
b) A lymphocytes: There is no specific type of lymphocyte referred to as "A lymphocytes." It is likely a typo or error.

c) T lymphocytes: T lymphocytes, or T cells, are another type of white blood cell involved in the immune response. T cells play a role in cell-mediated immunity, where they directly attack infected or abnormal cells. T cells do not directly produce antibodies.

d) B and T lymphocytes: While both B and T lymphocytes are involved in the immune response, only B lymphocytes are responsible for producing antibodies. T lymphocytes have different functions, such as recognizing and attacking infected cells or regulating the immune response.

questions.explanation: B lymphocytes are a type of white blood cells which produce antibodies, which protect the body against antigens.

questions.text: T-lymphocytes become mature and competent under the influence of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) Liver: The liver is an important organ in the body, but it is not involved in the maturation and competency of T-lymphocytes.
b) Bursa of Fabricius: The bursa of Fabricius is an organ found in birds that plays a role in the development and maturation of B-lymphocytes. It is not associated with T-lymphocyte maturation.

c) Thymus gland: This is the correct answer. T-lymphocytes, also known as T cells, mature and become competent in the thymus gland. The thymus is a primary lymphoid organ where T cells undergo differentiation and acquire their specific functions.
d) Spleen: The spleen is an organ that filters blood and plays a role in immune responses, but it is not directly involved in the maturation of T-lymphocytes.

questions.explanation: T-lymphocytes are a type of immune cells. They originate from stem cells in the bone marrow and are sent to the thymus gland for maturation which makes them immunocompetent.

questions.text: In passive immunity which of the following component are injected into blood?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Antigens stimulate the immune system to produce antibodies. In passive immunity, pre-formed antibodies are directly injected, not antigens that would require the immune system's response.
Immunogens are substances that trigger an immune response. Passive immunity involves the direct transfer of antibodies, not the activation of the immune system by immunogens.
Serum contains antibodies, as well as other components. In passive immunity, purified antibodies (immunoglobulins) are administered, not a mixture like serum, which can have unpredictable effects.
Immunoglobulins provide passive immunity as the body does not have to produce antibodies; instead, the injected antibodies offer a few months of protection.

questions.explanation: Immunoglobulins provide passive immunity as the body does not have to produce antibodies; instead, the injected antibodies offer a few months of protection.

questions.text: The luxury and well-decorated house, with its contents, _insured.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: 'Were' is used for plural subjects. Here, the main subject is singular ('house'), making this option incorrect.
'Have been' is used for plural subjects. The main subject 'house' is singular, so this option is incorrect.
“Is insured” is present simple passive, showing the house currently has insurance. It states a fact about the present.
“Has been insured” would imply the action of insuring happened in the past, but the focus here is on the current state of being insured

questions.explanation: “Is insured” is present simple passive, showing the house currently has insurance. It states a fact about the present.

questions.text: Find the error of article from the following sentenceAs a scientist, I would ask him to present a credible evidence of this belief.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is correct and introduces the role or identity of the speaker (a scientist).
This is correct and indicates a conditional or hypothetical action.
This article is unnecessary before "credible evidence" in this context. This is the correct option.
This is correct because "of" is the correct preposition to use in this context, indicating the relationship between the evidence and the belief.

questions.explanation: This article is unnecessary before "credible evidence" in this context. This is the correct option.

questions.text: Choose the correct option.
questions.subject: English

options.text: I have brought some footballs with that the children will play.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the placement of "with that" is grammatically wrong. The relative pronoun "that" should not follow the preposition "with" in this manner. Instead, "that" should be at the beginning of the clause, with the preposition at the end.

options.text: I have brought some footballs that the children will play with.
options.explanation: This option is correct. The relative pronoun "that" introduces the relative clause "that the children will play with," and the preposition "with" is correctly placed at the end of the clause.

options.text: I have brought some footballs with whom the children will play.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because "whom" is used for people, not inanimate objects like footballs. The correct relative pronoun for objects in this context is "that."

options.text: I have brought some footballs whose that the children will play with.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because "whose" is used to indicate possession, and it does not fit in this context. The structure "whose that" is also grammatically incorrect. The appropriate relative pronoun here is simply "that."

questions.explanation: This option is correct. The relative pronoun "that" introduces the relative clause "that the children will play with," and the preposition "with" is correctly placed at the end of the clause.

questions.text: Find the error of Punctuation.That is the secret of my extraordinary life, always do the unexpected.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The secret: This phrase is part of the first clause and doesn't require additional punctuation.
Extraordinary: This adjective is part of the first clause and doesn't require additional punctuation.

The sentence "That is the secret of my extraordinary life, always do the unexpected" is a compound sentence. To connect these two clauses, a conjunction or a semicolon is needed. However, in this case, a comma is used to separate the two clauses, making it a comma splice.To correct this, we can add a conjunction (like "and" or "which is") before "always do the unexpected", or change the comma to a semicolon.
The unexpected: This phrase is part of the second clause and doesn't require additional punctuation.

questions.explanation: The sentence "That is the secret of my extraordinary life, always do the unexpected" is a compound sentence, containing two independent clauses:1. "That is the secret of my extraordinary life" 2. "Always do the unexpected"To connect these two clauses, a conjunction or a semicolon is needed. However, in this case, a comma is used to separate the two clauses, making it a comma splice. To correct this, we can add a conjunction (like "and" or "which is") before "always do the unexpected", or change the comma to a semicolon.The corrected sentence would be: "That is the secret of my extraordinary life; always do the unexpected."

questions.text: Select the most suitable synonym for the given word:ASTOUNDED
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: It means competing or struggling for something.

It means extremely surprised or impressed.Astounded" means being shocked or surprised, often with a sense of wonder or amazement. Among the options, "amazed" is the closest in meaning, conveying a strong sense of surprise and impressment.
It means to give someone greater knowledge or understanding.
It means working together with someone to achieve a goal.

questions.explanation: "Astounded" means being shocked or surprised, often with a sense of wonder or amazement. Among the options, "amazed" is the closest in meaning, conveying a strong sense of surprise and impressment.

questions.text: Statements: Smoking is one of the human weaknesses, which tends to test the willpower of the smoker to the edge.Conclusions:I. It is very difficult for the smokers to give up smoking even if they want to.II. Human beings have other weaknesses as well.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Conclusion I follows because the statement emphasizes that smoking tests the smoker's willpower, suggesting difficulty in quitting.
Conclusion II follows because the statement refers to smoking as one of the human weaknesses, which implies the existence of other weaknesses.

options.text: Either conclusion I or II follows
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because both conclusions logically follow from the statement.

options.text: Both conclusions I and II follow
options.explanation: Both conclusions follow because the statement implies that smoking tests willpower (Conclusion I) and suggests there are other human weaknesses (Conclusion II).

questions.explanation: The phrase "tests the willpower to the edge" implies that quitting is very difficult (Conclusion I). The phrase "one of the human weaknesses" explicitly states that smoking is one among several weaknesses (Conclusion II). Both conclusions logically follow from the statement.

questions.text: Find the missing number in the series: 5, 7.5, 12.5, 22.5, ?
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: By following the pattern, each number is obtained by multiplying the previous number by 2 and then subtracting 2.5. Hence, 22.5 * 2 - 2.5 = 42.5.
This option does not follow the established pattern. Applying the pattern to 22.5 results in 42.5, not 42.
This option does not follow the established pattern. The calculation yields 42.5, not 44.
This option does not follow the established pattern. The correct result of the calculation is 42.5, not 43.5.

questions.explanation: The pattern is to multiply the previous number by 2 and then subtract 2.5. For example, 7.5 = (5 × 2) - 2.5 and 12.5 = (7.5 × 2) - 2.5. The missing term is (22.5 × 2) - 2.5 = 45 - 2.5 = 42.5.

questions.text: Statements: 1. All the offices on the 9th floor have wall-to-wall carpeting. 2. No wall-to-wall carpeting is pink. 3. None of the offices on the 9th floor has pink wall-to-wall carpeting. If the first two statements are true, the third statement is:
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Given that no wall-to-wall carpeting is pink and all offices on the 9th floor have wall-to-wall carpeting, it logically follows that none of these offices can have pink carpeting. Therefore, the third statement is true.
This option is incorrect because the first two statements logically support the conclusion that no office on the 9th floor has pink carpeting. Hence, the third statement cannot be false.
This option is incorrect because the first two statements provide a clear logical deduction, leaving no uncertainty regarding the truth of the third statement.
Since the correct answer is 'true,' this option is incorrect. The logical deduction from the given statements leads directly to the conclusion in the third statement.

questions.explanation: If every office on the 9th floor has a feature (wall-to-wall carpeting), and that feature can never be pink, then it is logically true that no office on that floor has pink wall-to-wall carpeting.

questions.text: Statement: The Income Tax authorities carried out raids at three different business houses in the city last week. Which of the following can be a possible effect of the above statement?
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: The three business houses will close down
options.explanation: While a raid indicates a serious investigation into financial irregularities, it does not automatically result in the permanent closure of a business. Raids are a method of gathering evidence, and legal proceedings or fines usually follow rather than immediate termination of operations.

options.text: The Income Tax department will stop raiding other business houses
options.explanation: This is incorrect because successful raids often lead to more investigations as authorities uncover broader networks of tax evasion. Executing one set of raids usually signals an increase in enforcement activity rather than the end of it.

options.text: Other business houses in the area will take corrective measures
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. High-profile enforcement actions by tax authorities serve as a strong deterrent to other local businesses. Seeing their peers raided often prompts other business owners to audit their own records and pay outstanding taxes to avoid similar scrutiny.

options.text: The government will change its tax policy
options.explanation: This is incorrect because raids are an administrative action used to enforce existing laws, not a reason to change the laws themselves. Tax policy changes are typically based on broad economic goals and national budget requirements rather than the outcome of three specific raids.

questions.explanation: When authorities take enforcement action against some businesses, a common and logical effect is that other businesses will review their own practices and take corrective measures to ensure compliance and avoid similar raids.

questions.text: Statement: There is an unprecedented increase in the migration of villagers to urban areas as repeated crop failure has put them into a precarious financial situation.Courses of Action:I. The villagers should be provided with an alternate source of income in their villages which will make them stay put.II. The migrated villagers should be provided with jobs in the urban areas to help them survive.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Providing an alternative source of income in villages addresses the root cause of migration, reducing the pressure on urban infrastructure.
Offering jobs to migrated villagers in urban areas does not address the underlying issue and could exacerbate urban challenges.
This option suggests that either course of action could independently resolve the issue, which is not the case.
Ignoring both strategies would not mitigate the migration issue, making it an ineffective stance.
While both actions could be beneficial, I is more effective in addressing the root cause of migration.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Only I follows. The primary issue is the migration of villagers due to crop failure. By providing an alternative source of income in the villages, the villagers will have less incentive to leave, thus reducing the strain on urban areas and addressing the root cause of migration. Option II, while helpful in the short term, does not address the underlying issue of rural distress and could lead to further strain on urban resources. Therefore, only Course of Action I is viable in the long term.

questions.text: A person got an infection, he became ill, but then he survived. What do you think of the immunity he would have developed?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Active immunity involves the body's own immune response to an antigen. However, this option is too broad without specifying how the immunity was acquired.

Artificially induced active immunity occurs when immunity is developed through vaccines, which introduce antigens in a controlled manner. This is not the case here, as the person was infected naturally.

Passive immunity originates from externally produced antibodies, such as those administered through antibody treatments or transferred through maternal antibodies. This immunity is not long-lasting and does not involve the person’s immune system actively responding to an infection.

Naturally induced active immunity occurs when the body generates an immune response after being exposed to a pathogen in a natural environment, resulting in the production of memory cells that provide long-term protection.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Naturally induced active immunity'. This type of immunity develops when a person is exposed to a pathogen through natural infection, prompting the immune system to produce antibodies and memory cells specific to that pathogen. This process leads to long-term immunity.Option A, 'Active immunity', is correct in describing the type of immunity, but it lacks specificity regarding how it was acquired.Option B, 'Artificially induced active immunity', involves immunity developed through vaccines, which is not applicable as the person was naturally exposed to the pathogen.Option C, 'Passive immunity', involves receiving antibodies from an external source, providing immediate but short-lived protection, unlike the body's own prolonged immune response.

questions.text: Average life span of red blood cell is about:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The average lifespan of a red blood cell (RBC) is approximately 120 days, which is equivalent to four months. RBCs play a crucial role in oxygen transport and are continuously produced in the bone marrow. After their lifespan, they are filtered out by the spleen and liver, with their iron content recycled for new RBC production.
This option is incorrect. While it is close to the correct lifespan, RBCs do not last five months; they typically last about 4 months or 120 days.
This option is also incorrect. A lifespan of two months is significantly shorter than the actual lifespan of red blood cells, which is around four months.
This option is incorrect as well. Red blood cells live much longer than one month; their average lifespan is about 120 days.

questions.explanation: The average lifespan of a red blood cell (RBC) is about 120 days or four months. RBCs are produced in the bone marrow and released into the bloodstream, where they perform the essential function of transporting oxygen from the lungs to the body's tissues. As RBCs age, they gradually lose their efficiency in oxygen transport, and after around 120 days, they are removed from circulation by the spleen and liver. The iron from the hemoglobin in these cells is recycled for the production of new red blood cells. The other options (five months, two months, and one month) do not accurately reflect the biological reality of red blood cell lifespan, which is consistently documented to be around four months.
